coredata.dc:title: Dual-functional ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on silica spheres for highly selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nouri, Maryam
Hajiaghababaei, Leila
Badiei, Alireza
Khalilian, Faezeh
Mazloomifar, Ali
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which validate the absence of ambient and excitation light intensity effects, have attracted significant attention. In this study, a novel nanohybrid dual-emission system was fabricated by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ). This system allows for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions by utilizing distinct signals. A ternary hollow structure nanosensor containing core-shell silica spheres modified by 8-HQ molecules and decorated with carbon quantum dots was fabricated by subsequent solvothermal synthesis. The designed nanohybrid emits dual-emission fluorescence peaks at 480 and 340 nm, originating from the intrinsic structure of NCQDs and 8-hydroxyquinoline, respectively. When Hg2+ is present, only the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm is noticeably reduced. However, in the presence of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm remains mostly unchanged, but clear fluorescence quenching is detected at 340 nm. There was a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios at 340 and 480 nm and the concentration of Hg2+ and Fe3+, with detection limits of 9.50 nM and 0.96 μM, respectively. Furthermore, it demonstrated satisfactory assay performance in quantifying the mercury content in amalgam alloy and the Fe3+ concentration in a water sample. The results of this study offer a new approach to designing multifunctional fluorescent sensors and a potential application for biomedicine and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon quantum dots
Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection
Core-shell silica sphere
8-hydroxyquinoline

Determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of the NCQDs
Synthesis and morphology characteristics hybrid sensors
Compositional characterization of the hybrid spherical sensors
Quantitative detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Reproducibility of the ion sensor and effect of pH towards Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectrum (a), Raman spectrum (b), XRD pattern (c) and XPS spectra (d) of the prepared NCQDs.
Flowchart diagram of the formation of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ nanohybrid system.
(a) SEM image of sSiO2@mSiO2, (b) TEM image of sSiO2@mSiO2, (c) SEM image of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ and (d) TEM image of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ.

(a) Small Angle XRD patterns of sSiO2@mSiO2, NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ; (b) Wide angle XRD pattern of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2; (c) Particle size distribution in aqueous solution of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ; (d) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of sSiO2@mSiO2, CQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ; (e) FTIR spectrum of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ; (f) Thermogravimetric analysis of sSiO2@mSiO2, NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ.
(a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized NCQDs, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs in diverse excitation wavelengths ranging from 270 nm to 350 nm.

(a) Emission spectra of NCQDs, 8-HQ and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ in the aqueous solution; and emission spectra of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ treated with Hg2+ and Fe3+ metal ions, (b) Comparison of the ratiometric emission intensity I340/I480 for NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ treated with different metal ions (c,d) The intensity ratios of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ sensor in the presence of 5 μM Hg2+ (Fe3+) mixed with 25 μM other metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ at different concentrations of Hg2+ (1–10 μM); and linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ nanohybrid and the Hg2+ concentration. (b) Fluorescence spectrum of the ratiometric probe at different concentrations of Fe3+ (5–50 μM) and linear relationship between I340/I480 of the ratiometric probe and the Fe3+concentration.

head.ce:title: Dual-functional ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on silica spheres for highly selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre Rey Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which validate the absence of ambient and excitation light intensity effects, have attracted significant attention. In this study, a novel nanohybrid dual-emission system was fabricated by combining nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) with 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ). This system allows for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions by utilizing distinct signals. A ternary hollow structure nanosensor containing core-shell silica spheres modified by 8-HQ molecules and decorated with carbon quantum dots was fabricated by subsequent solvothermal synthesis. The designed nanohybrid emits dual-emission fluorescence peaks at 480 and 340 nm, originating from the intrinsic structure of NCQDs and 8-hydroxyquinoline, respectively. When Hg2+ is present, only the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm is noticeably reduced. However, in the presence of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm remains mostly unchanged, but clear fluorescence quenching is detected at 340 nm. There was a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios at 340 and 480 nm and the concentration of Hg2+ and Fe3+, with detection limits of 9.50 nM and 0.96 μM, respectively. Furthermore, it demonstrated satisfactory assay performance in quantifying the mercury content in amalgam alloy and the Fe3+ concentration in a water sample. The results of this study offer a new approach to designing multifunctional fluorescent sensors and a potential application for biomedicine and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are highly recognized as one of the most hazardous types of pollutants that have harmful effects on ecosystems due to their speciation and bioaccumulation characteristics [1–3]. Mercury, one of the most toxic heavy metal ions, in the form of Hg (II), is a global concern owing to its human health risks and severe ecological damage [4–6]. Even at low concentrations, they can cause serious environmental pollution and human health issues, and are difficult to eliminate [7]. Due to the very poisonous nature of Hg2+, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) have mandated that the permissible limits of Hg2+ in water should not exceed 6 and 2 μg. L−1, respectively [8,9]. At the mention of Fe3+, it is the most abundant metal ions in living biological systems, and plays a vital role in oxygen transport, enzyme activity and binding with regulatory proteins [10,11]. Iron ions are essential in the biological system as they serve as binding sites that attract oxygen molecules from the air during the production of hemoglobin. Furthermore, iron plays a pivotal role in the creation of iron-sulfur clusters, which function as electron transfer groups, enabling redox reactions within the body [12,13]. However, an excess amount of iron ions can lead to serious health problems, including ischemia, anemia, and neurodegenerative diseases. Because of the special importance of these two metal ions, the requirement of developing a sensitive and selective method to detect Fe3+ and Hg2+ is extremely urgent [14–16].

Nowadays, several instrumental methods have been reported for the detection of metal ions from environmental samples, such as optical sensors, gas chromatography, fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry, X-ray fluorescence spectrometry, colorimetric assay, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, and cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry [17–19]. Although most of these analytical methods have attained satisfying detection limits and selectivity, they are restricted by complicated sample preparation, difficulty of operation, and expensive detection cost. In addition, drawbacks such as time-consuming processes and lack of portability [20,21]. Optical fluorescence sensors have recently attracted significant research attention due to their simplicity, on-site/in-field visual assay capabilities, and accessible utility characteristics, particularly for the detection of dangerous metal ions [22,23]. However, most chemosensors can only be used to detect one specific target ion. For instance, our group reported a fluorescent probe based on pyrimidines, which showed particular selectivity for Hg2+ ions [24,25]. Compared to single-emission sensors, dual-emission fluorescence probes can overcome inherent problems and eliminate background interferences with higher sensitivity [26–28]. Currently, the availability of optical probes with multiple signaling patterns is somewhat limited for the simultaneous measurement of two or more target analytes, particularly for various types of metal ions [29].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant interest as fluorescent nanomaterials due to their distinctive optical characteristics, favorable water solubility, strong durability, minimal toxicity, customizable fluorescence emission, exceptional biocompatibility, and minimal environmental harm [30,31]. Because of these excellent properties, CQDs were considered a promising nanomaterial for chemical sensing, biosensors, cell imaging, and medical diagnosis [32,33]. An inherent issue associated with employing CQDs probes in bioassays is their minute dimensions and remarkable water solubility, which facilitate their penetration across the cellular membrane and subsequent interaction with the nucleus [34]. Furthermore, chemical and colloidal instabilities limit the application of these fluorescence nanomaterials [35]. Consequently, it is crucial to comprehend how to guarantee one's own performance and uphold steadiness within the application process. Ordered mesoporous silica (OMS) is a highly favored inert substrate for depositing fluorescent nanoparticles on surfaces due to its several distinct benefits over other materials [36,37]. Because of its large specific surface area and pore volume, as well as its variable pore size, it is ideal for encapsulating small-scale nanomaterials. In addition, leakage can be avoided due to the resistance of silica to both aqueous and non-aqueous solvents [7]. Deposition of CQDs on the surface of the OMS not only stabilizes the CQDs, but also does not interfere with their excitation and emission because the OMS is optically clear [38]. Furthermore, silica is biocompatible and can be easily changed with functionalized groups to provide a dual emission fluorescence probe.

Based on the aforementioned considerations, a novel dual-emission fluorescent probe, designated NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ, has been synthesized for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. This probe was developed through the in-situ growth of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) on silica core-shell spheres via a hydrothermal method. The coupling of 8-hydroxyquinoline molecules to the surface of sSiO2@mSiO2 occurred through covalent bonds, resulting in the successful preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes that exhibit emission peaks at 340 nm and 480 nm, under a single excitation wavelength of 300 nm. In the presence of Hg2+, there is a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm. Conversely, when Fe3+ ions are present, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm remains largely unchanged, while a notable quenching is observed at 340 nm. Consequently, the ratio of the two fluorescence intensities in this ratiometric fluorescent probe alters, facilitating the quantitative detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. This research offers an effective strategy for the preparation of dual-emission photoluminescent materials utilizing a stepwise synthesis approach for the simultaneous detection of multiple metal ions.
The Supplementary Information contains all the details regarding the apparatus utilized.

All the chemical reagents used in the experiments were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck companies and were used as received without further purification. The following chemicals were used: ammonia (≥ 99 %, 17.03 g/mol), ethanol (≥ 99.8 %, 46.07 g/mol), tetraethyl orthosilicate (≥ 98 %, 208.33 g/mol), n-octadecyltrimethoxysilane (≥ 95 %, 374.68 g/mol), citric acid (≥ 99.5 %, 192.12 g/mol), o-phenylenediamine (≥ 99 %, 108.14 g/mol), 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonic acid (≥ 98 %, 225.22 g/mol), chlorosulfonic acid (≥ 99 %, 116.52 g/mol), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (≥ 98 %, 221.37 g/mol), dichloromethane (≥ 99.8 %, 84.93 g/mol), and triethylamine (≥ 99.5 %, 101.19 g/mol). Additionally, various nitrate metal salts (≥ 98 % purity) were used to prepare stock solutions for fluorescence experiments. Water is purified with a Milli-Q system. (≥ 18 MU.cm).

The modified Stöber method [39] was used to create a monodisperse silica structure with a solid core and a mesoporous shell (sSiO2@mSiO2). After stirring for 25 minutes at 30 °C, 4.1 ml of aqueous ammonia (32 wt%) and 15 ml of distilled water were added to 70 ml ethanol to make a solution. The aforementioned solution was then mixed vigorously with 5.8 ml tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and left stationary for 1 hour to generate monodispersed solid SiO2 spheres. A combination of TEOS (4.8 ml) and n-octadecyltrimethoxysilane (C18TMOS (1.9 ml)) was applied dropwise under stirring and maintained stationary at room temperature for 4 h to form a homogenous mesoporous shell around the dense cores. The resultant mixture was centrifuged, dried at 60 °C, then calcined in an air environment at 550 °C for 6 h at a rate of 5 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A one-pot hydrothermal method was used to synthesis NCQDs according to the previously reported approach with slight modification [40]. Briefly, 0.54 g of citric acid and 0.25 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in distilled water (15.0 ml) to form a clear solution. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave with 20 ml capacity and heated at 210 °C for 6 h. The synthesized NCQDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. After that, the supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis bag for 24 h to remove the residue. Finally, the NCQDs solution was diluted with 15 ml of anhydrous ethanol and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The as synthesized core-shell SiO2 powder (0.5 g) and 15 ml of ethanol were added to a flask. One milliliter of the prepared NCQD solution was then added under magnetic stirring, and 150 µL of ammonia solution (32 %) was added dropwise. The solution was stirred for 1 h. A suspension was obtained after aging for 24 h at 25 °C. Finally, the suspension was separated, washed with 20 ml ethanol several times, and dried at 70 °C for 24 h.

The synthesis of 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonylchloride was carried out following the procedures described in our previous publication [41]. A standard procedure involved combining 8 mmol of 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonic acid with 6 ml of chlorosulfonic acid in a 100 ml round-bottom flask. Following 24 h of stirring at ambient temperature, the resulting mixture was transferred into ice water. The resulting yellow precipitate was then filtered and dried at 70 °C to get 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonylchloride.

To synthesize 8-HQ functionalized NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2, the NCQDs modified core-shell silica was first functionalized with aminopropyl groups using aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). Then, the amino groups on the surface were reacted with 8‑hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonylchloride to obtain the final product. Specifically, approximately 0.2 g of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 were placed into a 100 ml flask containing 70 ml of 100 % ethanol, while maintaining an argon environment. Subsequently, 0.3 ml of APTES was added to the resulting mixture. Following an 18-hour reflux, the resulting mixture underwent filtration and was subsequently washed with ethanol three times. It was then dried in ambient air and designated as NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/NH2. 0.2 g of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots supported on mesoporous silica coated with aminopropyl-functionalized mesoporous silica was introduced into a round bottom flask containing 80 ml of dichloromethane, while maintaining an argon environment. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 18 h after adding an excessive amount of 8-hydroxyquinoline-5-sulfonylchloride (0.240 g, 1.0 mmol) and triethylamine (2.0 mmol). Ultimately, the obtained sample was gathered, subjected to multiple washes with 30 ml dichloromethane and 40 ml ethanol, and subsequently dried in an oven at a temperature of 50 °C for a duration of 24 h to get the ultimate product.

For all fluorescence experiments, 0.005 g of nanosensor in 100 ml of distilled water was dispersed using an ultrasonic bath. Appropriate nitrate salts were dissolved in deionized water to make stock solutions of metal ions (1 × 10−2 mol L−1). 2 ml of the prepared solution and 50 µL of different metal ions (10−2 M) were utilized to record the fluorescence spectrum. A ratio of 1:5 was used to add other ions as interfering agents to the 2 ml prepared solutions in the competition tests. In addition, for the titration tests, various amounts of the metal ion were added to the 2 ml of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ dispersion. The fluorescence measurements were performed with the excitation wavelength at 300 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the amount of mercury present in an amalgam, a precise procedure was followed. The amalgam comes in the form of small balls, each weighing 1 g. One of these balls was dissolved in 1:1 HNO3/H2O, and the mixture was boiled until the amalgam was entirely dissolved. The resulting solution was then transferred to a 1000 ml calibrated flask to reach 1000 ppm amalgam solution. By diluting the mother solution, appropriate sample solutions were obtained. The proposed optical sensor was then used to determine the amount of mercury in the sample solution, with the help of the calibration method. Furthermore, the performance of the present method for real water sample analysis was evaluated using drinking water for Fe3+ detection in an artificial system. Without pretreatment, drinking water samples were spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions solution and then analyzed with the proposed probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional group and surface structure of the synthesized NCQDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 ( Fig. 1a). The synthesized NCQDs are highly water soluble because of the existence of the carboxyl groups on their surface, which can be analyzed from the FTIR spectrum. The two peaks at 3378 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 are owing to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of -OH, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 1130 cm−1 and 1608 cm−1 are attributed to C-O and CO stretching vibrations. The existence of apparent absorption peaks at 1577 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of NCQDs corresponds to N-H and C-N bending vibrations. These peaks are consistent with the available literature and confirm the water solubility of NCQDs [42]. Raman spectroscopy is a valuable tool for the identification of edge defects, disorder and in plane crystalline size of graphitic carbon. As shown in Fig. 1b, the D-band and G-band were detected in the range of 1300–1400 cm−1 and 1500–1600 cm−1, respectively. The sharp peak of G-band represents sp3 defects (hybridized) while the weak peak of D-band reflects sp2 hybrid carbons. The intensity ratio between the crystalline D band and the disordered G band was estimated to be 0.76, exhibiting a similar structure to graphite [43]. Fig. 1c represents the diffraction pattern of synthesized NCQDs from 0 to 80°, indicating the typical disordered graphitic structure, which is consistent with the reported literature for NCQDs [44]. CQDs with nitrogen-containing functional groups were further tested by the XPS analysis. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra results of NCQDs are exhibited in Fig. 1. As is shown in Figure d (i), the XPS spectra of NCQDS illustrated strong peaks of C 1 s at 284.7 eV, O 1 s at 531.7 eV, and an obvious N 1 s signal at 400.9 eV, which proved the successful nitrogen doping in CQDs. The XPS of the C1s level demonstrated the presence of three peaks centered at including sp2/sp3 carbon (C-C, CC), C-N, C-O, and CO at 284.7, 285.2, 286.1, and 288.4 eV, respectively (Fig. d (ii)). The XPS fitting of O1s indicated two peaks centered at 530.65 and 532.3 eV associated oxygen in the CO and C-OH/C-O-C in the CQDs (Fig. d (iii)). In addition, the XPS fitting of N1s level exhibited double peaks at 399.3 eV (C-N-C) and 400.4 eV ((C)3-N), which further verifies the as-synthesized CQDs are nitrogen-doped (Fig. d (iv)). In sum, XPS strongly showed that the synthesized CQDs contain oxygen and nitrogen atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphology characteristics hybrid sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication process of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ nanohybrid sensor is illustrated in Fig. 2 and involves four main steps: (i) The starting material used was monodispersed spherical SiO2, which was synthesized. (ii) The silica cores were coated with uniform mesoporous shells of silica to create a core-shell silica structure. (iii) Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots were in-situ decorated on the surface of sSiO2@mSiO2 using a hydrothermal process. (iv) A highly active fluorophore group was assembled on the surface of the mesoporous shell to create a dual emission fluorescent sensor architecture.

The surface morphology and size of the as-prepared samples were observed by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) ( Fig. 3). A scanning electron microscopic image (Fig. 3a) indicates that the core-shell silica particles prepared using the one-step Stober approach have a quite uniform spherical morphology. The TEM image (Fig. 3b) reveals that the surface of the solid silica cores with diameters of about 220 nm was coated with mesoporous silica shells with diameters of about 30 nm to form core-shell nanoparticles. Fig. 3c shows the scaffold structure maintains spherical morphology after being decorated with NCQDs and modified with 8-HQ molecules. From the TEM image, it can be observed that numerous ultrafine NCQDs are dispersed randomly on the outside surfaces of core-shell silica in the final nanostructure (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compositional characterization of the hybrid spherical sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Small angle powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed to examine the mesoporous structure of the as prepared nanoparticles ( Fig. 4a). The silica core-shell particles indicate a definite diffraction peak at 2.1°, which is typical of wormhole mesostructures rather than long-range mesopore channels. The presence of a solid core without porosity might account for the absence of well-defined high order diffractions and the relatively broad primary diffraction. The peak intensity for pure core-shell silica spheres was slightly decreased upon decoration with NCQDs, demonstrating that the introduction of amorphous nanoparticles affected the pore structure. After surface functionalization with 8-HQ molecules, the peak intensity decreased again, which was predictable. The high angle XRD pattern of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 (Fig. 4b) also displayed a broad peak centered at the same position as those of NCQDs, which is very similar for amorphous silica. These results prove that as expected both NCQDs and sSiO2@mSiO2 are amorphous in nature.

Fig. 4c indicates the particle size distribution of a nanohybrid sensor, which was measured using the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique. According to this distribution, the average size of sensor particles is about 250 nm, which is in consistence with electron microscopy results.

Nitrogen gas porosimetry was tested for the characterization of the surface area and porosity of sSiO2@mSiO2, NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2, and NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ composites. Table S1 indicates the detailed surface parameters for various spherical samples. From Fig. 4d, it can be seen that all the samples show type IV according to the IUPAC classification, certifying the mesoporous network. The support sample synthesized without 8-HQ modification or NCQDs decoration shows the highest surface area compared to other samples. The specific surface area of sSiO2@mSiO2 decreases slightly after embedding with NCQDs and further modification with the fluorophore group which is mainly caused by the blocking of the channel structure. Nevertheless, the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ composites still possess a high surface area and pore volume and abundant mesoporous channels, which are beneficial for their sensing applications.

In addition, the structural information of the as-prepared samples was characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 4e). The obtained results show that the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 mainly contains Si-O-Si bonds (1066, 800 and 457 cm−1) and a carbon species (1637 cm−1 of CC) grafted with oxygen-functional groups (954, 1211, 1405, 1735 cm−1 for Si-OH, C-OH, OC-O, CO, respectively). More interestingly, the Si-O-C vibrations appeared at 1150 cm−1, which exhibits the covalent bond between the carbon particles and the silica structure. This observation demonstrates the phenomenon that the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 powder shows a completely white color to the naked eye, although it contains a high amount of carbon particles. In addition, the surface of nanocrystalline quantum dots (NCQDs) is enriched with numerous carboxyl groups, while the fractures of silica in sSiO2@mSiO2 exhibit hydroxyl (OH) groups. Consequently, the process of esterification during hydrothermal treatment is facilitated, leading to the chemical combination of carbon and silica in the final product. This process of carbon grafting is supported by the Si 2p spectrum of the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 (Fig. S1) and further confirmed through the FTIR findings. In the FT-IR spectra of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ, N-H stretching vibrations of amino groups appear at 3420 and 3280 cm−1 and N-H bending vibrations appear at 1630 cm−1. The bands at 2860 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 are owing to the symmetric and asymmetric vibration of the CH2 group in the propyl chain. Furthermore, CN and CC band vibrations of the 8-HQ ring can be detected at 1630, 1550, 1500, 1470 and 1387 cm−1 and the band at 1334 cm−1 can be referred as asymmetric vibration of OS=O group. To conclude, these results demonstrated successful covalent grafting of organic moieties onto the hollow silica surface.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of sSiO2@mSiO2 demonstrates a consistent decrease in weight between 40 °C and 800 °C, exhibiting a gradual slope (Fig. 4f). The remarkable thermal durability of sSiO2@mSiO2 is confirmed by the mere 2 % reduction in weight percentage observed up to a temperature of 800 °C. The specified decrease relates to the evaporation of water and moisture that is absorbed in the sample cavities, as well as the removal of hydrogen or hydroxyl groups from the surface. According to the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curve of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2, shown in Fig. 4f, as the sample temperature is raised to 300 °C, the mass percentage of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 gradually drops with a little incline. Specifically, as the temperature rises from 40 °C to 150 °C, the weight percentage of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 drops by approximately 2 %. This decrease is due to the evaporation of moisture that has been absorbed within the pores and on the surface of the nanocomposite. At a temperature of 300 °C, the weight percentage of the sample reduces by approximately 4 % compared to the weight percentage at 150 °C. This reduction can be attributed to the thermal degradation of the NCQDs within the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 composite. In addition, the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ sample exhibits a significantly higher level of degradation compared to the NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 sample. This is due to the presence of 8-HQ groups on the surface of the spheres, which are prone to thermal breakdown at elevated temperatures. By elevating the temperature to 300 °C, the weight percentage of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ experiences a reduction of over 10 % in comparison to the mass percentage of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2 at the identical temperature gradient. Based on these numbers, approximately 4 % of NCQDs and 10 % of 8-HQ have been applied to the sSiO2@mSiO2 substrate.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of NCQDs ( Fig. 5a) shows a weak absorbing peak at about 225 nm accompanied by a strong peak around 320 nm. The absorbing peak at about 225 nm can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds, while the wide attributed to peak at 320 nm can be assigned to the n-π∗ transition of CO on the surface of NCQDs. Furthermore, NCQDs showed blue photoluminescence with excitation-independent emission behavior (Fig. 5b). Although fluorescence emission intensities were alerted, emission peak positions did not indicate any shift under the different excitation wavelengths, indicating that blue emissions of quantum dots originate from more uniform size and surface defects of NCQDs. NCQDs could have a maximum emission peak at 480 nm with the excitation wavelength at 300 nm.

Pure NCQDs and 8-HQ species indicated an emission peak at 480 and 340 nm. At the same excitation wavelength (300 nm), the as-prepared NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ hybrid spheres showed dual interesting, well-resolved emission peaks at 340 nm and 480 nm ( Fig. 6a). As a result, it can be concluded that the emission band around 480 nm is from NCQDs structure and the emission peak around 340 nm can arise from 8-HQ molecules. Considering that the synthetic NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ possessed a variety of excellent characteristics, such as strong dual-emission fluorescence emission, interesting structure with high surface area and fluorescence stability, the fluorescence response of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ to metal ions in aqueous solution was investigated at room temperature. For this purpose, nanohybrid sensors were dispersed into aqueous solutions of different metal cations (Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Hg2+ and Co2+) and fluorescence emission was obtained. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ at 480 nm was almost completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+, whereas the fluorescence of 8-HQ molecules was not alerted. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of at 340 nm was almost completely quenched in the presence of Fe3+, whereas the fluorescence of at 480 nm has barely alerted (Fig. 6b). The selectivity of the sensor was estimated by separately adding various metal ions to the sensor, and the results are shown in Fig. 6c and d. As can be seen, the intensity ratios changed significantly by adding Hg2+ and Fe3+, while these ratios presented unobvious inhibition by adding other metal ions with a 5-fold concentration of Hg2+ or Fe3+, indicating the high selectivity of the sensor toward Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. To further reveal the excellent selection of our developed sensor, competition experiments were performed in the presence of Hg2+ or Fe3+ mixed with other metal ions. Fig. 6d illustrated that the intensity ratio of our sensor in the presence of 5 μM Hg2+ (Fe3+) mixed with 25 μM other metal ions was similar to that of in the presence of 5 μM Hg2+ (Fe3+) alone. The results demonstrated that the presence of other metal ions did not influence the detection of Hg2+ or Fe3+ using our sensor. This confirms that our ratiometric fluorescence probe offers superior analytical accuracy compared to single-channel detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7a, in the absence of metal ions, the ratiometric sensor emitted two well-resolved emission peaks centered at 340 and 480 nm. Upon addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm decreased until quenching while the intensity at 340 nm remained constant. The value of I480/I340 indicates a linear relationship with the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 1–6 μM, with a linear regression equation of I480/I340 = 0.0235 C + 21.33 (C: Hg2+ concentration, μM) and a correlation coefficient of 0.996 (inset curve in Fig. 7a). Using the 3σ rule, the detection limit was obtained to be about 9.5 nM (σ = S0/S, S0 is the standard deviation of the blank solution after repeated measurements, and S is the slope of the standard curve). Table S2 compares this detection limit with that of other methods used in other studies. The comparison result indicates that the prepared nanohybrid system in this study can reduce interference effects caused by excitation light, the environment, and sensor concentration changes.

In addition to Hg2+ ions, we found that Fe3+ ions could quench slightly the fluorescence emission peak at 340 nm, while the fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm changed significantly in the presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7b). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity ratio I340/I480 decreased about 5 folds after addition of 50 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7b). The ratio I340/I480 response to other metal ions was little even in the presence of a 2-fold concentration of other metal cations. The selective response to Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions indicates that the prepared NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ has the potential to be an interesting ratiometric sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions simultaneously. In detail, we systematically analyzed the influence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of a nanohybrid sensor. As shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence emission peak at 340 nm of NCQDs/sSiO2@mSiO2/8-HQ exhibited a dramatically decrease as the concentration of Fe3+ ions gradually increased. In contrast, the fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm changed slightly as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased. The curve in Fig. 7b demonstrates a clear linear correlation between the ratio I340/I480 and the concentration of Fe3+ ions, ranging from 5 to 50 μmol L−1. The detection limit was measured to be 0.96 μM (3σ rule), which was not only significantly below the highest acceptable concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water approved by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (5.4 μM), but also lower than the maximum level stipulated by the European Union (3.6 μM) [45]. The synthesis of the core-shell silica sphere featuring a solid core and a mesoporous shell presents a high specific surface area, which significantly enhances sensitivity in this study. This structural design facilitates further modifications using NCQDs and allows for covalent functionalization with 8-HQ, thereby establishing a unique ratiometric fluorescent platform. This innovative approach, which integrates the extensive surface area of the mesoporous shell with the dual-emission characteristics of NCQDs and 8-HQ, enables the highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe³⁺ and Hg²⁺ ions. This structure and capability have not been previously documented in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility of the ion sensor and effect of pH towards Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the reproducibility of the prepared sensor was evaluated by measuring the target Hg2+ at 1.0 × 10−5 M with 6 replicates. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of them reached 0.65 %, indicating that the proposed sensor had satisfactory stability and reliability. Furthermore, the pH value of the sensor solution has a significant influence on fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency. Fig. S2 displays the effects of pH (5.0–9.0) on the fluorescence intensity ratio for Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions. The ratio between fluorescence intensities reaches a maximum at pH=7. The ratio fluorescence intensity decreases gradually with pH from 7 to 9. Thus, pH=7 was selected as the optimal pH value for fluorescence detection. Hence, fluorescence measurements were carried out in neutral aqueous solutions.

The optical sensor designed to selectively detect Hg2+ proved to be effective as a selective sensor. Its application was analyzed for the determination of the mercury content amalgam alloy using direct fluorometry. The results were compared with data from inductively coupled plasma (ICP) spectrometry measurements (as shown in Table S3). The data in Table S3 indicates that the Hg2+ sensor's mercury content reading for the Alloy sample is in good agreement with those obtained by ICP measurements. There was no discernible statistical disparity between the sensor and ICP outcomes. Furthermore, the sensor's assessment of the recovery study for spiking Fe3+ in drinking water yielded favorable outcomes, as indicated in Table S4. Hence, the current investigation appears to be beneficial for the identification of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in actual samples.

To sum up, a fascinating approach was presented to fabricate a dual-emission fluorescent sensor based on hollow silica spheres modified by 8-HQ molecules and decorated with NCQDs for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Hg2+ in aqueous solution. A rapid and specific fluorescent response to Hg2+ is observed in NCQDs at 480 nm and Fe3+ in 8-HQ at 340 nm. Due to its inert nature, it is not affected by possible interference from substances such as metal ions from other sources. When Hg2+ was presented in the hybrid sensor of NCQDs and 8-HQ, the fluorescence of NCQDs was quenched and the fluorescence of 8-HQ was not changed. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, only the emission wavelength centered at 340 was quenched and the fluorescence of NCQDs was not changed. More importantly, the low detection limit of Hg2+ can be estimated by the ratios of NCQDs-based emission as the recognition part and 8-HQ-based emission as the reference part and the opposite situation happens to Fe3+ ions. Such a nanohybrid ratiometric fluorescence system will provide guidance for designing other ratiometric fluorescence assays with broad sensing applications. The satisfactory results of analyzing a series of water and amalgam samples further validate the practical applications of the proposed sensor.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Alireza Badiei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Leila Hajiaghababaei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Maryam Nouri: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Mazloomifar: Writing – review & editing. Faezeh Khalilian: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Islamic Azad University of Yadegar-e-Imam Khomeini (RAH) Shahre-rey Branch and the University of Tehran for assisting this research financially.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135755.
